political.s: <cite index="3-4">With annual revenue exceeding $50 billion</cite>, Tyson is a major political donor. The FEC data shows right-leaning preference with $452K raised and 73.8% Republican donations (15500/21000). The company's involvement in agricultural policy and industry regulation aligns with typical meat industry conservative positioning.

charity.s: <cite index="22-25,22-26,22-27">Tyson committed $2.5 million to fight hunger in 2023, partnering with Feeding America member food banks and donating more than 40 million pounds of protein to regional food banks and community pantry partners nationwide</cite>. <cite index="24-1">The company has donated more than 43 million pounds of protein, valued at approximately $50 million, directly to the food bank network over the last three years</cite>.
charity.focus: Food insecurity, hunger relief, rural food access

environment.s: <cite index="3-1">Tyson Foods released at least 371.7 million pounds of pollution into U.S. waterways between 2018 and 2022</cite>. <cite index="6-1">The company has incurred more than $13.178 million in penalties for environmental violations since 2010</cite>. <cite index="10-1">The company's environmental record includes a history of Clean Water Act violations, with a notable incident resulting in a $2 million criminal fine by the DOJ in 2018 for a spill that killed over 100,000 fish in Missouri</cite>.

labor.s: <cite index="16-3,16-4">Tyson agreed to a nationwide injunction requiring the company to pay poultry processing workers for all hours worked, and agreed to pay almost 3,000 workers at its Blountsville, Alabama facility $500,000 in overtime back wages under the Fair Labor Standards Act</cite>. <cite index="20-18,20-19">During COVID-19, workers at Tyson's Waterloo, Iowa pork processing plant were forced to work without protections, standing elbow-to-elbow, most without masks</cite>. <cite index="44-6,44-9">The median salary for its 132,950 employees in 2025 was $43,206, while CEO compensation reached $34.47 million, reflecting a 798:1 pay ratio</cite>.

dei.s: <cite index="34-3,34-4">Tyson announced it would join McDonald's Mutual Commitment to Diversity, Equity and Inclusion program, committing to actions such as setting representation goals, conducting education and training, increasing diverse supplier spend and developing innovative programs and partnerships to increase diversity and inclusion across its business</cite>. However, <cite index="35-7,35-8">Tyson Foods deleted over 30 DEI posts after Trump's 2024 win, with critics calling it a PR stunt to cover labor abuse and immigration controversies</cite>.

animals.s: Not applicable - Tyson Foods is a meat processor, not a pharmaceutical or cosmetic company. The company raises and processes livestock for food production.

guns.s: Not applicable - Tyson Foods is a meat processing company and does not manufacture, sell, or distribute firearms.

privacy.s: Limited public information available regarding Tyson Foods' data privacy practices. The company processes employee data for payroll and operations but no comprehensive privacy policy evaluation is publicly available. Standard corporate privacy practices assumed but not formally assessed.

execPay.s: <cite index="44-11,44-9">CEO Donnie King's compensation reached $34.47 million in 2025, an increase from $22.77 million in 2024, with a CEO-to-median-employee pay ratio of 798:1</cite>. <cite index="46-2">CEO compensation rose 51% to $34.5 million, fueled by a 197.8% funded short-term cash bonus and substantial one-time equity awards, occurring despite the prior three-year long-term incentive plan failing entirely to vest</cite>.

fec.committeeName: TYSON FOODS INC POLITICAL ACTION COMMITTEE (TYPAC)

sec.note: Pay ratio extracted from proxy filing — verify manually for accuracy
